input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A lab-scale gantry crane system consisting of a physical crane structure and real-time controller, designed for industrial automation research and education. Its measurable operational parameters and modular design facilitate validation of digital twin methodologies including continuous validation, fault detection, and optimal control.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Services include optimal control for trajectory optimization, historical data logging for system performance tracking, real-time data visualization for monitoring crane operations, continuous validation against physical system behavior, and fault detection mechanisms. These services support researchers and educators in validating digital twin approaches and conducting reproducible experiments.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation utilizes Python (gantrylib module for C4 Containers), Docker for containerization of TimescaleDB, Eclipse Mosquitto MQTT broker, and Grafana components, TimescaleDB for time-series data storage, Grafana for data visualization, Eclipse Mosquitto as the MQTT messaging broker, and Autodesk Fusion360 for CAD modeling of the physical crane.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system architecture is visualized using the C4 Model, with a TimescaleDB database for time-series data storage and Eclipse Mosquitto as the message broker. It includes a Simulation Service for predictive modeling and a Continuous Validation Service that compares real-time sensor data with digital twin outputs to ensure accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Lab Scale Gantry Crane system employs a TimescaleDB database for time-series data management and an MQTT broker for real-time communication between physical and digital components. The digital twin utilizes a kinematic model to simulate crane movements, with sensor data streams synchronized to the physical system for dynamic state updates.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: A Continuous Validation Service compares executed trajectory measurements with simulations, calculating distance metrics against an acceptable threshold to ensure model accuracy and trigger corrective actions when discrepancies exceed predefined limits.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from the Physical Crane via a Python-based Physical Crane Interface to the Logging Application, which continuously logs crane state data into a TimescaleDB database. Specific communication protocols, data types beyond 'crane state', preprocessing steps, and data quality assurance mechanisms are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands are sent from the Human Machine Interface to the Control Application, which uses the Physical Crane Interface to execute movements on the physical crane. Command types, message formats, validation, authentication, feedback systems, and execution confirmation details are not provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The digital twin services communicate using an MQTT broker for signaling. Network topology, bandwidth, latency requirements, reliability mechanisms, and security protocols are not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The Digital Twin system is deployed on-premise on a personal computer, with the Database, MQTT Broker, and Dashboard services containerized using Docker. Computational and storage requirements are handled by the host machine, though specific scalability mechanisms and availability assurances are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The system employs an ad-hoc development approach without a commercial digital twin framework. Hardware components include an Arduino microcontroller for sensor measurement and control, running on a personal computer. The implementation uses open-source technologies like TimescaleDB for time-series data storage and Grafana for visualization, though none are specifically designed for digital twin applications.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The system provides data logging, real-time visualization of crane operations, optimal trajectory generation for puck movement, and continuous validation of system behavior through sensor feedback to ensure accurate container handling.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system does not adhere to any specific digital twin standards or frameworks; it uses general open-source technologies such as TimescaleDB and Grafana, which are not tailored for digital twin applications, as stated in Section V.E: 'We make use of open-source technologies, but none of them are tailored specifically to digital twins'.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the lab-scale gantry crane, its physical components (crane and controller), and its purpose for research and education. It correctly identifies the key methodologies (continuous validation, fault detection, optimal control) for which the system serves as an exemplar. Quotes: 'The physical side consists of the physical crane and its controller.', 'used for future research or education on the topic of digital twins.', 'demonstrator for the following digital twin related research: continuous validation methods, fault detection methods...'

The extraction correctly lists the core services mentioned in the text, such as optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization, and continuous validation. However, it lists 'fault detection mechanisms' as a service, whereas the source text categorizes fault detection specifically as a research application or method demonstrated using the system, rather than a standard service container like the others. Despite this slight conflation, the description is otherwise highly specific and well-supported. Quotes: 'provides services for optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization and continuous validation.', 'Dashboard: a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from the database.', 'Continuous Validation Service: a service that continuously compares measurements...'

The extracted description correctly identifies tools such as Python (gantrylib), Docker, TimescaleDB, Grafana, Eclipse Mosquitto, and Autodesk Fusion360. The sources confirm these: 'gantrylib' (Section III.B), 'Docker' (Section III.B), 'TimescaleDB' (Section III.B), 'Grafana' (Section III.B), 'Eclipse Mosquitto' (Section III.B), and 'Autodesk Fusion360' (Section III.C). The score is 5 because the extraction is comprehensive, specific, and fully supported by the sources.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

The document describes the internal system architecture and mentions reliance on 'freely available and commonly used software', but does not discuss integration with external enterprise systems, data exchange protocols, or interoperability standards. block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the specific technologies and models used in the digital twin. It correctly cites the use of TimescaleDB, an MQTT broker, and a kinematic model for trajectory generation and simulation. The description of data logging and state updates is supported by the text's description of the logging application and the general data flow.
quotes: MQTT Broker: an MQTT broker for signaling between services
Trajectory Generator Service: a service that utilizes a kinematics model of the crane

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source documents explicitly describe the physical actuators. The text mentions: 'Two Motors and associated Controllers, one to control the cart’s movement, one to control the hoisting movement' and 'An Electromagnet at the end of the hoist to pick up containers'. Since evidence exists in the text, the claim that it is missing is incorrect.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source documents explicitly identify the sensing infrastructure. The text lists: 'A Swing Angle Encoder measures the swinging motion of the hoist, and an Anemometer measures the wind speed'. Since this information is present in the documents, the claim that it is missing is incorrect.

The extraction is highly accurate and specific, perfectly matching the description of the Continuous Validation Service in the source. It correctly details the comparison of measurements and simulations, the use of distance metrics and thresholds, and the resulting operator notifications.
quotes: Continuous Validation Service: a service that continuously compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories
It calculates distance metrics between them and compares the value with an acceptable threshold
In case the threshold is breached operators are notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

The extraction correctly describes the control path from the Human Machine Interface to the Control Application and then to the physical crane. It accurately identifies the missing details regarding command formats and validation. Supported by: 'Control Application: a Python application that uses the crane interface to control the crane' and 'Human Machine Interface: a Python front-end for the operator to configure and control the crane.'

The extraction correctly identifies the MQTT broker as the signaling mechanism between services and accurately notes the lack of detailed network specifications in the text. Supported by: 'MQTT Broker: an MQTT broker for signaling between services.'

Provides a highly specific summary of the deployment infrastructure: on-premise on a personal computer, with specific services (Database, MQTT Broker, Dashboard) containerized using Docker. It correctly identifies the host machine handles computational requirements and notes the lack of scalability mechanisms in the description. Supported by quotes: 'plug in the physical crane to a personal computer, and run all of the software on that computer', 'the Database, MQTT Broker and Dashboard C4 Containers are containerized with Docker'.

block_5_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.
